title: Harness: measurement frameworks haven't caught up with AI productivity, eroding developer trust
summary: Harness's State of Engineering Excellence 2026, fielded in April across 700 practitioners and managers in the US, UK, India, France and Germany, finds the top three challenges are measurement-of-productivity (26%), maintaining code quality with AI (24%), and proving ROI (18%). 54% of developers fear individual performance evaluation based on AI usage data; managers are nearly 4x more likely than practitioners (15% vs 4%) to say they have no concerns about how AI data is used. Developers want separation between improvement data and evaluation (55%), measurement transparency (50%), and a voice in defining metrics (49%). The data shows a widening manager–practitioner trust gap that is becoming a retention and adoption risk.
whyItMatters: The 4x manager–practitioner gap on AI-data concerns is a leading indicator of trust erosion; ignoring it now creates retention exposure later.
If 54% fear AI usage being used in evals, voluntary AI adoption data will become biased, breaking the same metrics leaders rely on.
Demand for measurement co-design (49%) is a concrete, low-cost lever to rebuild trust before mandating tools.
recommendedActions: Publish an explicit policy separating AI-improvement telemetry from individual performance evaluation.
Co-design AI productivity metrics with practitioner representatives before rolling out exec dashboards.
Pair every productivity KPI with a cognitive-load or burnout indicator to detect over-optimization.
risksAndCaveats: Vendor-sponsored survey (Harness sells DevOps tooling) — directional, not unbiased.
Self-reported fear/trust questions are sensitive to framing.
Geographic mix tilts Western; norms around evaluation-by-data differ in other markets.
